title: Low-Energy Purification of Crystal Defects by Rydberg Excitons
authors: Shiva Kant Tiwari, Tijs Karman, Valentin Walther
abstract: Recent experiments show that optically generated Rydberg excitons in cuprous oxide can neutralize charged impurities, strongly reducing stray electric fields and effectively purifying the crystal. Here, we develop a multichannel theory of Rydberg exciton-impurity scattering that resolves the competing roles of capture, elastic scattering, and inelastic transitions between excitonic states. We find that at high collision energies, as effective under conventional single-photon excitation, purification is reduced relative to Langevin capture. These collisions are accompanied by inelastic redistribution and dominant elastic scattering, including pronounced glory scattering, which suppress purification efficiency. We identify a quantum regime at ultralow collision energies favorable for purification, where only the s-wave contributes: capture is enhanced while elastic and inelastic channels are strongly suppressed. This regime can be accessed via degenerate two-photon excitation of even-parity Rydberg excitons with tunable recoil, additionally enabling the systematic exploration of exciton-impurity scattering over a wide range of collision energies beyond what is readily achievable in atomic counterparts in atomic gas experiments.
summary: The paper presents a multichannel theory to explain how Rydberg excitons can be used to purify crystals by neutralizing charged impurities. It demonstrates that while high-energy collisions can hinder purification through inelastic scattering, an ultralow-energy quantum regime exists that maximizes purification efficiency. This regime can be experimentally accessed using tunable two-photon excitation in cuprous oxide.
main_problem: Understanding the competing mechanisms of capture, elastic scattering, and inelastic transitions during Rydberg exciton-impurity collisions and how they affect crystal purification efficiency.
main_result: The authors identify a quantum regime at ultralow collision energies where s-wave scattering enhances capture while suppressing loss channels, making purification more efficient than at high collision energies.
method: Development of a multichannel theory of Rydberg exciton-impurity scattering.
model_or_system: Rydberg excitons in cuprous oxide (Cu2O) interacting with charged impurities.
key_observables: Purification efficiency, capture rate, elastic scattering cross-section, and inelastic transition rates.
important_parameters: Collision energy, s-wave scattering regime, and even-parity Rydberg exciton states via two-photon excitation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of impurity neutralization, presents a multichannel scattering theory, compares high-energy vs. low-energy collision regimes, and proposes a method for accessing the optimal purification regime via two-photon excitation.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical framework for controlling impurity-driven decoherence and purification in solid-state systems using Rydberg-like physics, bridging the gap between atomic physics techniques and condensed matter applications.
